does: This paper introduces MoR, a federated alignment framework for Vision-Language Models that replaces traditional parameter aggregation with preference-based collaboration. Rather than sharing model parameters or raw data across clients, each client locally trains a reward model from preference annotations on their private data. MoR combines GRPO (a reinforcement learning approach) with a Mixture-of-Rewards mechanism featuring learned routing that adaptively fuses heterogeneous client reward models based on input characteristics. The framework enables decentralized training and alignment of VLMs while preserving privacy and accommodating clients with different computational resources, application requirements, and model architectures, addressing the extreme heterogeneity challenge in federated settings.
connects: This work builds on federated learning research that addresses privacy-preserving decentralized training, extending beyond standard parameter aggregation approaches like FedAvg to handle architectural and data heterogeneity. It connects to recent work on reward modeling and preference-based learning as an alternative to direct supervision, and draws on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) methods applied to language models. The use of mixture-of-experts style routing mechanisms adapts techniques successful in other domains to the federated setting. The paper also responds to challenges in aligning heterogeneous foundation models across distributed clients without exposing private data or requiring compatible architectures, building on earlier federated approaches for multi-client scenarios with varying capabilities.
enables: This framework opens pathways for privacy-preserving deployment of VLMs in regulated domains like healthcare and finance where data cannot be centralized. It enables future work on more sophisticated preference aggregation mechanisms and adaptive routing strategies for heterogeneous reward signals. The preference-based collaboration interface could generalize beyond VLMs to other foundation models, potentially supporting federated alignment of diverse architectures simultaneously. Future directions include developing theoretical guarantees for preference-based federation, scaling to larger numbers of heterogeneous clients, and exploring applications where clients have conflicting preferences or objectives that require balancing rather than simple fusion.
